title: SEGMENT 10 — Environmental metagenomics: Tara Oceans and the planetary microbiome

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Environmental metagenomics: Tara Oceans and the planetary microbiome
Welcome back.
Let's leave the human body for a while, because the microbiome story is much bigger than humans.

The same culture-independent DNA sequencing approach that revealed the human microbiome has been applied to every environment we can sample — the ocean, soil, freshwater, deep subsurface, hot springs, ice cores, the atmosphere, the built environment — and what it has revealed is that microbes are everywhere, more diverse than we ever imagined, and doing planetary-scale chemistry that shapes everything from ocean productivity to atmospheric composition to the carbon cycle to the nitrogen cycle.
The microbial world is the substrate on which all macroscopic life operates, and metagenomics is finally letting us see it.

The single most ambitious environmental metagenomics project of the past two decades is probably Tara Oceans, an expedition led by Eric Karsenti, Chris Bowler, and many collaborators, with key bioinformatics led by Shinichi Sunagawa at ETH Zurich and Peer Bork at EMBL Heidelberg.

From 2009 to 2013, a research schooner called Tara sailed around the world, stopping at over 200 stations across all the major ocean basins, and at each station, sampling seawater at multiple depths — surface, deep chlorophyll maximum, mesopelagic — and filtering out everything from viruses to large plankton through a series of size fractions.
The samples were preserved and shipped back to dozens of labs around the world for analysis.
The result was the first systematic catalog of microbial and viral diversity in the global ocean.
The Tara Oceans dataset, published in a famous bundle of papers in Science in 2015, was staggering.
They identified roughly 40 million novel microbial genes, the vast majority of them previously unknown.
They constructed thousands of metagenome-assembled genomes of marine bacteria, archaea, and protists.
They mapped the global distribution of microbial functions — nitrogen fixation, photosynthesis, sulfur cycling — and showed how those functions vary with latitude, depth, and ocean chemistry.
They identified temperature as the single biggest predictor of marine microbial community composition, with major implications for how the ocean's biogeochemistry will respond to climate change.
Subsequent extensions — Tara Polar, Tara Pacific — have expanded the coverage to polar and reef environments.
The dominant microbes in the open ocean are not the ones you'd guess from textbook microbiology.
The most abundant photosynthetic organism on Earth is a tiny cyanobacterium called **Prochlorococcus**, discovered by Sallie Chisholm and her collaborators in the late 1980s.
Prochlorococcus cells are smaller than most bacteria — under a micron across — and they thrive in the nutrient-poor open ocean by being extraordinarily efficient at low light and low nutrients.
There are estimated to be around 10^27 Prochlorococcus cells in the ocean, collectively responsible for something like 5% of global photosynthesis.

They were essentially unknown until forty years ago, and their genomic diversity — multiple ecotypes adapted to different light and depth regimes — has been a textbook case for studying microbial ecology and evolution.
Closely related is **Synechococcus**, another marine cyanobacterium with similarly global distribution.
The most abundant heterotrophic group in the ocean is the SAR11 clade, named after a clone library from Sargasso Sea sample 11, where they were first identified by 16S sequencing in 1990.

SAR11 bacteria — the best-known of which is Pelagibacter ubique — make up around a third of all bacterial cells in surface ocean waters and are perhaps the most numerous organism on the planet, with a global population on the order of 10^28 cells.
They eat dissolved organic carbon at incredibly low concentrations and recycle it back into the marine food web.

SAR11 was identified by 16S long before anyone managed to culture it; the first cultured representative wasn't isolated until 2002, and even then, it required highly specialized low-nutrient conditions.

They are a perfect example of the unculturable-then-culturable trajectory: discovered by metagenomics, characterized phylogenetically, eventually grown in a lab once we understood enough about their biology to figure out what they need.
Beyond the ocean, soil metagenomics has revealed perhaps the most complex microbial communities on Earth.
A single gram of soil can contain tens of thousands of microbial species and several billion cells.

Soil microbiomes drive most of the terrestrial carbon and nitrogen cycles, mediate plant nutrient uptake through symbioses with mycorrhizal fungi and nitrogen-fixing bacteria, and process essentially everything that lives, dies, and decays on land.

Large efforts like the Earth Microbiome Project, founded by Rob Knight, Jack Gilbert, and Janet Jansson in 2010, have sampled tens of thousands of environmental sites — soils from every continent, sediments, plant rhizospheres, animal guts — and built a global picture of how environmental conditions shape microbial communities.
The Earth Microbiome Project standardized the 16S V4 sequencing protocol that became the field's default, and its database is now a foundational reference for environmental microbiome studies.

The deep subsurface — the rocks kilometers beneath the surface, both on land and under the ocean floor — has its own microbiome, dominated by slow-growing chemolithoautotrophs that get energy from inorganic chemistry (oxidizing hydrogen, reducing iron, fixing carbon from CO2).
Some of these organisms have generation times of years to centuries, lurking in the rock matrix at low cell densities, doing chemistry on geological timescales.
The total biomass of the deep subsurface biosphere is estimated to rival or exceed that of all surface life.
Most of it has never been seen alive in a lab.
We know it's there only because of DNA pulled out of drill cores and amplified.
The take-home from environmental metagenomics: most microbial diversity on Earth lives nowhere near us.
The vast majority of microbial cells, genes, and metabolic functions are in oceans, soils, sediments, and the deep subsurface, doing chemistry that runs the biogeochemical cycles of the planet.
The human microbiome, fascinating as it is, is a tiny corner of the global microbial world.
Metagenomics is the only tool that gives us access to the rest.
And that access has profound implications not just for understanding the planet but for finding new useful molecules, which is where we go next.
